title: Integer Representation and ASCII
summary: How computers represent positive integers and text characters in binary.
content_html:
Computers store all data — numbers, text, images — as binary. How we interpret the bits determines what the data means.

Unsigned Integers

An unsigned n-bit integer can hold values from 0 to 2ⁿ−1:

  • 8 bits → 0 to 255
  • 16 bits → 0 to 65,535
  • 32 bits → 0 to 4,294,967,295

ASCII Character Encoding

ASCII (American Standard Code for Information Interchange) encodes text characters as 7-bit codes (128 characters). Key values to know:

'A' = 65 = 0x41
'a' = 97 = 0x61
'0' = 48 = 0x30
' ' = 32 = 0x20

Extended ASCII uses 8 bits (256 characters) for additional symbols.

Multi-byte Data

Groups of bits represent different things depending on context:

32-bit pattern 0x41424344 could be:
  As int:    1094861636
  As string: "ABCD"
  As float:  ~12.141 (IEEE 754)

Common Mistake

The computer doesn't know what the bits represent — the programmer (or program) decides the interpretation. A float and int stored as the same bit pattern mean completely different things.

exercises.title: ASCII A
exercises.prompt: What is the ASCII code for the uppercase letter 'A'?

exercises.title: 8-bit range
exercises.prompt: What is the maximum unsigned value storable in 8 bits?

exercises.title: 16-bit max
exercises.prompt: How many unique values can a 16-bit unsigned integer represent?

exercises.title: Context interpretation
exercises.prompt: The same 32-bit pattern can be interpreted as an integer, float, or string. What determines the interpretation?

exercises.prompt: What is the ASCII code for the character '0' (zero)?

exercises.prompt: Extended ASCII uses how many bits?

exercises.prompt: What is the smallest addressable unit of memory?

exercises.title: Hex 0x41
